sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Acute rhinosinusitis is usually viral and self-limiting. Prehospital care should focus on symptom relief, identification of orbital or intracranial complications, and appropriate escalation for severe, prolonged, or worsening illness.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Facial pain or pressure, often worse bending forward
- Nasal blockage or purulent nasal discharge
- Reduced smell, headache, or maxillary toothache
- Fever or malaise in some cases

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Most cases do not need antibiotics
- Red flags include periorbital swelling or erythema, visual change, severe frontal headache, persistent vomiting, focal neurology, altered mental status, or meningism
- Allergic rhinitis, dental disease, and migraine can mimic sinus symptoms

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey only if clinically indicated, then focused assessment
- Consider simple analgesia
- Saline irrigation or a short course of topical decongestant may be helpful where appropriate
- Encourage hydration and rest
- Provide review advice if symptoms persist beyond the expected course or significantly worsen

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Escalate for any orbital, neurological, or systemic complication features
- Doctor-directed antibiotics may be appropriate when symptoms are prolonged, worsening after initial improvement, or accompanied by significant systemic features
- Stronger analgesia may be considered within scope if required

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Dust, smoke, and environmental irritation may aggravate symptoms
- Maxillary tooth pain does not always mean primary dental disease, but dental causes should be considered
